Can I call to, and thank you so much for the opportunity

a bold bill that can make disability rights in New Zealand.
Um, I, I, I hope you understand
that accessibility is a pre condition for the fulfillment

Many of you take for granted when you have an impairment.
And here in Otero, one in six people,
around 900,000 people have a disability.
And every day, every day we face barriers at schools,
public spaces in online at work.
And accessibility is not just a disability. Sure.
It, it benefits everyone.
We know that parents and friends
and seniors, children, tourists,
and anyone with a temporary injury.
And yet we continue to design systems that exclude.
And, and this exclusion comes at a really personal cost,
but it also cost our economy billions in lost potential.
So, I don't want to repeat myself,
but what we need is a comprehensive legislation in co-design
with disabled people, aka Maori funnel
and the deaf community.
And we need, and this is, it's already written, we just kind
of need to copy and paste.
We need to translate the UN CRPD
into domestic legislation
and make accessibility a legal right
with enforceable standards.
We did an independent oversight, um, body to monitor
and enforce compliance, a way
that people can notify when they face barriers in a free

Process. And of course, we need a national campaign

And I will pass now to Dr.
Claire Flemer, and then Neil will speak

that the committee might have for us.
Thank you, Dr. Claire. To you,
Bye. Thank you. I'm
Claire Flemer from Matthew University School
of Built Environment, doing research onto the accessibility
of buildings for people with disability.
The current NZS 4 1 2, 1 is 23 years old,
and it urgency needs revision reflecting universal
design principles.
I have five brief points to make on this one.
We have to stop thinking that disability is something
that happens to a small group of people in New Zealand.
It will affect every one of us.
That means you, me, a friend, a family member at some point,
either through a medical condition and accident
or as we age.
So it's really important we view this as a whole
of New Zealand issue.
Point two, who's going to pay for improved accessibility?
The answer is every taxpayer
with a social program like the New Zealand pensions,
because that's the only way to spread the cost fairly
amongst everyone.
With government subsidies for accessible features,
we will achieve better building access
for people with disabilities.
Point three, how do we go forward
and what are the priorities?
We've surveyed New Zealand people with many different types
of disabilities, and they've told us exactly which buildings
are the hardest to access
and what are the worst accessibility problems.
So we can provide a clear list of priorities
for improvement, recognizing that we can't fix everything,
but we've got to make a start.
Point four, how do we monitor
and enforce inclusive access for public buildings?
We add the accessible features to the building,
warranted fitness, the bwo
alongside other critical systems like the
fire safety systems.
The building then has annual inspections
and fine for non-compliance.
Finally, we don't expect our pensioners to demonstrate
that they are worthy of receiving a pension.
And in the same way, we shouldn't expect our people
with disabilities to prove that they are worthy of being
included in society.
They deserve the same quality of life as everyone else.
They should be able to participate in society
and not be stuck at home alone and depressed
because they can't access buildings.
Thank you.
And my name is, uh, Neil Jaris. And Kiara, everybody.
Thank you so much for the opportunity to speak to you all.
Um, I just want to make a couple of brief points as well

why this really matters to, to us.
And I'm gonna focus on the area that I know well.
I work in the field of digital accessibility, access
to information, that kind of thing.
And there, there are a couple of very real examples
that I have experienced
and that would be echoed many, many times
by people in our community.
Uh, so they're not just ones that affect me.
I remember when, um, I was coming back to new,
to New Zealand in 2022.
It was that brief period
that year when the borders were, were open.
And, um, I was coming back to New Zealand
and I was minutes away from being unable to board my flight.
Not because of anything I had done, not
'cause I was late to the airport, but
because the health declaration that we quite rightly had
to fill in at that time, uh, in order to, to be able
to reenter the country, was totally inaccessible to me.
Mm-hmm. Whether that was on the website
or on my mobile phone.
Now that was unforgivable,
especially since it was a government department
that was supposed to be, uh, uh, driven
by the web accessibility guidelines that already existed.
Flash forward to today, that still continues.
Um, you can now come home to New Zealand and,
and fill out an online declaration form, uh,
rather than the old paper one
that, that we are used to doing.
But if you do that, you can only do that on
the, on the website.
If you are a screen reader, user like myself, um,
you cannot use the app even though the app
is available to everybody else.
It's inaccessible. It's a government
department, it's not right.
And I suspect that the reason for that is
that the current web accessibility guidelines
that the government, um, is we trying to enforce, uh, only,
uh, refer to, uh, websites.
They don't refer to mobile apps,
but how many of us rely on mobile apps these days rather

And lastly, I would say, uh,
this is echoed throughout society,
not just government departments,
but um, local government as well.
And, uh, and the private sector.
And so even with the improved web accessibility standards
that currently are, are being introduced, it's not enough.
'cause they only apply to central government.
They don't apply to the rest of the economy.
And that's a real problem. So the problems
that we are having are as a result of the failure of
the noble effort to try
and persuade people to do the right thing.
And that is why we need legislation.
And we're happy to take any
questions that people might have.

in the buildings act and then maybe within
kind of order as well?

into disability rights in New Zealand.

that will generate barriers.
Because the problem is that we, at the moment,
we are constantly putting actually public money

And that cost way more money.
So if we start doing things, uh, right from the start,

and a lot of other people comes like,

I ask you to make a reflection

Because it costs so much to keep this,
this status quo right now, not only in money,

You. Uh,
I'm Rebecca Alvi, uh, DCE Outreach

Uh, my sign name is, which I think is referenced

Thank you to the committee for inviting FHA
to present on these two petitions.
Improving accessibility
for Disabled New Zealanders is a key focus for F kaha.
Last year, the Minister for Disability Issues,
minister Upton decided not to go forward
with the accessibility for New Zealanders bill.
The minister considered that the bill as drafted was not fit
for purpose, and that while legislation can be an enabling
factor, the most effective means
of improving accessibility are likely
to be non-GI legislative.
Instead of progressing legislation,
the minister asked fa Kaha
to develop an accessibility work program
to make tangible improvements
to accessibility in New Zealand.
This work program is based on the idea that most people
and most organizations either want
to do the right thing in terms of accessibility
but don't know how, or they're not aware of the ways that
what they are doing is creating barriers for disabled people
to access or participate.
The work program consists of a mix of shorter
and longer term initiatives focused on raising awareness
and making it easy for people
and society to do the right thing with regards
to access for disabled people.
The work program is structured around four key areas
where accessibility can be improved, the built environment.
And I'm really happy to let those online know that we've
recently agreed that there will be a review
of NZS 4 1 21 in the, um, near future, uh,
digital and information accessibility, transport
and emergency management and response.
There's also another category for initiatives
that don't naturally fit within these, uh, such
as guide dogs, which can span built environment
and transport.
We're also working with community representatives,
including some people on this call, uh,
to establish an advisory group to help f kaha
with the identification, prioritization
and delivery of these initiatives.
Alongside this, f kaha is leading a nu a number
of other activities that will improve accessibility.
We are currently refreshing the New Zealand disability
strategy with input from the disability community.
The five priority outcome areas
of the refresh strategy are education, employment, health,
housing, and justice.
Accessibility is a key issue which runs throughout all
of those outcome areas.
The working groups for each outcome area include officials,
community members, and industry representatives.
Many working group members have expert knowledge on some
of the key accessibility issues in those areas.
F kaha is also refreshing the accessibility charter.
This is an all of government commitment
to work progressively to ensure all public information is
accessible and that disabled people can interact
with government services in ways that meet their needs.
When F Kaha was established
as a new standalone ministry in December last year,
we took on responsibility for the government provision
of information in alternate formats.
FHA is currently stabilizing
and enhancing the alternate format
service and collaborating.
Collaborating with the Department of Internal Affairs
to make improvements in digital accessibility as well
as we accessibility, FHA is committed
to supporting disabled people
to be involved in government decision making.
We facilitate opportunities for disabled people, t Maori
and their whanau to contribute to key areas
of work across government in a number of ways.
We fund the Disabled People's Organization's Coalition
and its role as a member
of the independent monitoring mechanism or IMM
and the IMM promotes protects
and monitors the implementation
of the United Nations Convention on the rights
of Persons with disabilities.
The DPO Coalition is a powerful voice
for disabled people across government.
FAHA is also supported by a strategic advisory group
with membership from across a broad range
of disability organizations, including some
that are not members of the DPO Coalition.
This advisory group is intended to challenge our thinking,
help us identify ways, uh, areas for improvement,
and to create ways for diverse disabled voices,
including those of family whanau and carers to be heard.
The refresh of the New Zealand Disability Strategy,
which we mentioned earlier,
is being undertaken in collaboration with working groups
that include members of the disability community.
The wider disability community will be consulted on the
draft strategy within the coming months,
and we welcome feedback from disabled people.
We also administer a nominations database which collects
expressions of interest from disabled people who
with relevant skills and experience.
So they can be considered for nominations
for relevant governance and advisory positions on state
sector boards and committees.
We are also working with the Public Service Commission on a
four point plan that will support the creation
of employment opportunities within the public
service for disabled people.
This can support more disabled people being included in
government decision making processes.
Thank you.
Thank you very much. Just have one clarifying question

the disability survey, and it asks a series

which within which people have some form of impairment.
Right. And it identified approximately 850,000 New
Zealanders, uh, meet the conditions within those questions.
Okay. Right. Thank you very much. Other questions?
I'm interested to understand.
Um, I think this work is amazing.
Firstly, really, um, advocate
for nothing about us without us
and having meaningful, um, participation

Um, or 90%. A hundred percent.
Um, because if it's in legislation, then it has to happen.

He was institutionalized because the home wasn't accessible.

A a around not legislating for it, do
You? Yeah,
thanks. So I guess it's really about, um, uh,

'cause they do require, you know, ongoing commitments

I guess the government of the day makes decisions on, um,

Um, I suppose the other point is that, um, uh,
legislation is not needed
to enable something like that to happen.
Um, so, uh, I guess, um, the

Mm-hmm. And I guess what we're doing

enable improvements
to accessibility like Rebecca's outlined.
Okay. Jan, Thanks and lovely to see you, Helen.
Um, I just really want to sub on that, is
that the accessibility bill,

Um, so I guess that's the, the kind

But I think what the minister articulated
as her rationale was that, um,
she felt it was better to take a step back from legislation

and try to focus on more concrete,
tangible steps that could be taken.
And that's what she's asked us.
Can I just second on that?
So that being the case, in your opinion with the groups

Because it seems to me that there's still this desire

to be enabling legislation.
Because from what I can see and, and read

it, it can get overseen.

Is there going to be a move towards legislation?

Because it doesn't seem to be that that's the case?

that we evaluate the accessibility work
program, uh, as we go.

into the work program at the moment.
Um, because I think the untapped opportunities

Um, and my own experience tells me most people do want

because they either don't know or they don't know how.
Um, and there are lots of ways that, um,
things can be improved.

that don't require legislation.

but they don't have to do they,
so those councils can choose to do that or not?

or not that's in legislation. Hmm.

Yeah, sure. Yeah. Yeah.

So we cannot ask public servants to respond why the,
it it hasn't been place.
But the other thing is
what we need is a total change in culture and values.
Mm-hmm. Um, I truly believe that why New Zealand is so far
behind in terms of disability rights, um, is
because we live in a very ableist society.
For some, for some reason, we still think here as a society
that having a disability make you worse off, make you less.
It's quite confronting. But we need to have disco,
courageous conversations.
Why do you think that it's okay that I need to go

Because I, I'm in a wheelchair.
Why no one take a stand in this, this is not okay.
Why someone like Neil cannot go online
and make his shopping like everybody else was cited.
And it's just fine, you know?
So we need to kind of why, why being treated not

It's not okay. I have a dear friend who's

She say that if people without impairments put up

But we prefer non-violent resistance,

And, and I truly believe that legislation

Yeah. Right. That's not how, okay.

We should do all of the things

Okay. Okay. Sure. Can I miss

yeah. Um, the week

this was one of their findings.

if it arrives at all often comes
